title: CHAPTER 3 —
CONSTRAINTS AND BOUNDARIES
segments.body_html:
A researcher asks Claude to summarize the evidence on a contested medical topic. Claude returns four paragraphs: confide

Chapter 3 — Constraints and Boundaries — The prompt never said to use only the provided sources. The prompt never said what to do with uncertainty. Claude perfor

beats.text: A researcher asks Claude to summarize the evidence on a contested medical topic. Claude returns four paragraphs: confident, well-organized, full of specific claims. The researcher skims it, finds it plausible, and pastes it into a draft. Two days later, a colleague points out that one of the cited effects does not appear in any of the papers the researcher actually provided. Claude had filled the gap with something that sounded reasonable. The prompt never said to use only the provided sources.

segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK

segments.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO
title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS
YOU DO.
segments.body_html:
·Define the scope of a task so Claude stays inside the job and does not invent adjacent material
·Set evidence rules so Claude draws only on what you provide
·Require uncertainty disclosure when evidence is missing or incomplete
·Protect private or sensitive data from appearing in outputs
·Specify a tone or register that must not drift

beats.text: After reading this chapter, you will be able to write prompts that: Define the scope of a task so Claude stays inside the job and does not invent adjacent material Set evidence rules so Claude draws only on what you provide Require uncertainty disclosure when evidence is missing or incomplete Protect private or sensitive data from appearing in outputs Specify a tone or register that must not drift Give Claude a clear instruction for what to do when a constraint conflicts with the request

segments.label: 02 / 12 · WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

title: WHAT A
CONSTRAINT IS.
segments.body_html: Scope constraints→Evidence constraints→Uncertainty constraints→Privacy and ethics constraints→Tone and register constraints

beats.text: A constraint is any part of a prompt that limits what Claude does rather than specifying what Claude produces. The task says "write a summary." The constraint says "only from the three documents below, flagging anything that is not directly supported." Scope constraints define what is inside the task and what is not. "Summarize only the methodology section." "Cover only cost, not timeline." "Do not include recommendations , only findings."

segments.label: 03 / 12 · WHAT A CONSTRAINT IS

title: BEFORE AND AFTER:
ADDING CONSTRAINTS TO A PROMPT
segments.body_html:
·Whether it should confine itself to sources the user provides
·What to do when evidence is mixed or contested
·Whether the user wants confidence levels or just conclusions
·How long the output should be
·Whether "productivity" means self-reported satisfaction, output measurement, or something else

beats.text: Summarize the research on remote work and productivity. What Claude produces: A confident multi-paragraph summary drawing on Claude's broad training, including studies, meta-analyses, and observations from across years and contexts. The output will be fluent and organized. It will also contain specific claims about effect sizes, dates, and findings that the user cannot verify , because no sources were provided and no constraints were set.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · BEFORE AND AFTER: ADDING CONST

title: THE "DO NOT
INVENT" INSTRUCTION.
segments.body_html:
·"If a specific fact, figure, or name is not in the materials I have provided, do not include it."
·"If you would need to guess, say 'I do not have a source for this' and stop."
·"Do not add context from your training that was not given in this prompt."
·"If you are uncertain about a claim, mark it with and I will verify it."

beats.text: One constraint earns its own section: telling Claude not to fabricate. Claude does not lie in the conventional sense. It generates text that is statistically coherent with its training. When it does not know something, it sometimes produces plausible-sounding text anyway , a phenomenon widely called hallucination in the research literature (though the mechanism is more precisely described as next-token prediction operating beyond the limits of grounded knowledge). The NIST AI Risk Management Framework (2023) identifies this as a key reliability risk for AI systems in professional contexts.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · THE "DO NOT INVENT" INSTRUCTIO

title: CONSTRAINTS ON REFUSAL:
WHAT TO DO AT THE BOUNDARY
segments.body_html: "Refuse or ask" rule:→"Flag and continue" rule:→"Narrow the scope" rule:

beats.text: A constraint is not useful if Claude does not know what to do when it cannot comply. Suppose you instruct Claude to use only provided sources, but the task cannot be completed with those sources alone. What should Claude do? The default, without instruction, is to fill the gap. With explicit instruction, Claude can do something more useful.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · CONSTRAINTS ON REFUSAL: WHAT T

title: CONSTRAINTS THAT
CONFLICT.
segments.body_html: Resolution by priority:→Resolution by exception:→Resolution by query:

beats.text: Occasionally you will write constraints that pull against each other. "Be comprehensive" and "stay under 200 words" will conflict whenever comprehensiveness requires more space. "Use only provided sources" and "define all technical terms" will conflict if the sources do not define those terms. When constraints conflict, Claude will typically pick one and proceed, often prioritizing the most recent or most specific instruction. A better approach is to anticipate common conflicts and specify a resolution order.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · CONSTRAINTS THAT CONFLICT

title: THE HUMAN
GATE.
segments.body_html:
·Is every claim in these materials traceable to a provided source?
·Is every uncertain claim flagged?
·Is the output within the specified word count?
·Does the tone hold throughout?
·Has any private information appeared in the output?

beats.text: Constraints do not remove the need for review. They make review easier. A constrained prompt produces output that can be checked against the constraint list. Each item on that list is a review question:

segments.label: 08 / 12 · THE HUMAN GATE

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Stating the task without any constraints.→Using constraints that are too vague to enforce.→Setting too many constraints without resolution order.→Assuming Claude remembers constraints from earlier in a conversation.→Treating a constraint as a substitution for judgment.

beats.text: Stating the task without any constraints. "Write a policy brief on this topic" is a task. A policy brief specification also states who the audience is, what sources count as evidence, how long it should be, and what it must not claim. Missing any of these opens the door to drift. Using constraints that are too vague to enforce. "Be accurate" is not a constraint , it is an aspiration. "Draw only on the following four reports and mark any claim not traceable to them" is a constraint.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · COMMON MISTAKES

title: BRIDGE TO
CHAPTER 4.
segments.body_html: You now have the tools to limit what Claude does. The next question is how to show Claude what right looks like. Constra

beats.text: You now have the tools to limit what Claude does. The next question is how to show Claude what right looks like. Constraints close doors. Examples open a specific one. Chapter 4 introduces few-shot examples, counterexamples, and style guidance , the part of a specification that teaches by demonstration rather than prohibition.
